topic: A person's manuscript is rejected by their dream publisher
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the quiet finality of a closing door. *Regretfully declined.* My hands, which had been trembling slightly as I reread the polite, impersonal rejection, felt suddenly cold. I stared at the screen, the glow reflecting in my wide, unblinking eyes. For a full minute, nothing moved. Then, a sharp, involuntary intake of breath escaped me, followed by a slow, deliberate push of the chair back from my desk. I walked to the window, pressing my forehead against the cool glass. Outside, the city hummed with indifferent energy, but inside, the silence felt vast and heavy, pressing down on my chest. I picked up my pen, the cheap plastic feeling alien in my grip, and found myself tracing the outline of a forgotten sentence on a blank page, the ink refusing to flow.

[story 2]

"No." The word echoed in the small apartment, a hollow sound that seemed to vibrate in the floorboards. I reread the rejection letter three times, each time the words seemed sharper, more dismissive. My jaw tightened until a dull ache bloomed beneath my ear. I paced the length of the living room, my footsteps muffled by the thick carpet, a frantic, repetitive rhythm. I stopped abruptly by the bookshelf, running my fingers over the spines of my past successes, a strange mixture of pride and acute deflation washing over me. I leaned my head back against the wall, closing my eyes tightly, trying to steady the rapid flutter in my stomach. When I opened them, the world looked too bright, too sharp, and I couldn't seem to focus on anything but the stark white of the rejection notice still lingering on my mind.

[story 3]

I kept replaying the editor's note in my head: "Lacks market viability." It was a sterile judgment, a verdict delivered without a single moment of engagement with the actual prose. A knot formed just below my sternum, tightening with every passing second. I stood by the kitchen counter, gripping the edge until my knuckles were white. I needed to do something, anything, to disrupt the stillness that had settled over me. I snatched my laptop, intending to start a new project immediately, but my fingers hovered over the keyboard. Instead, I just stared at the screen, the cursor blinking mockingly in the void, feeling utterly paralyzed, as if the very air had become too thick to draw a full breath.
